epigraph.text:
A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a 
just weight is his delight.

content.text:
Two fractions can look different but represent the same amount. Just as 
a half dollar and two quarters have the same value, $\frac{1}{2}$ and 
$\frac{2}{4}$ represent the same portion of a whole. Understanding this 
equivalence is essential for working with fractions.

content.title: Equivalent Fractions
content.content:
**Equivalent fractions** are fractions 
that represent the same value. They name the same point on a number line.

content.title: Visualizing equivalence

content.text:
The key principle for creating equivalent fractions is simple: multiply 
(or divide) both the numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number.

content.title: Creating Equivalent Fractions
content.content:
For any fraction $\frac{a}{b}$ and any nonzero number $n$:
\[
\frac{a}{b} = \frac{a \times n}{b \times n}
\]
This works because multiplying by $\frac{n}{n} = 1$ doesn't change 
the value.

content.title: Building equivalent fractions
content.solution:
Multiply numerator and denominator by 2, 3, and 4:
$$
\frac{3}{4} &= \frac{3 \times 2}{4 \times 2} = \frac{6}{8} \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{4} &= \frac{3 \times 3}{4 \times 3} = \frac{9}{12} \\[6pt]
\frac{3}{4} &= \frac{3 \times 4}{4 \times 4} = \frac{12}{16}
$$
So $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{6}{8} = \frac{9}{12} = \frac{12}{16}$.

content.title: Finding a specific equivalent
content.solution:
We need to find what number to multiply 5 by to get 20:
\[
5 \times ? = 20 \implies ? = 4
\]
So multiply both numerator and denominator by 4:
\[
\frac{2}{5} = \frac{2 \times 4}{5 \times 4} = \frac{8}{20}
\]
The missing number is 8.

content.text:
Just as we can make fractions with larger numbers, we can also reduce 
them to simpler forms. A fraction is in **simplest form** (or 
**lowest terms**) when the numerator and denominator have no 
common factors other than 1.

content.title: Simplest Form
content.content:
A fraction is in **simplest form** (or 
**lowest terms**) when the greatest common factor (GCF) of the 
numerator and denominator is 1.

content.title: Simplifying Fractions
content.content:
1. Find the GCF of the numerator and denominator.

2. Divide both the numerator and denominator by the GCF.

3. The result is the fraction in simplest form.

content.title: Simplifying a fraction
content.solution:
Step 1: Find the GCF of 12 and 18.

- Factors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12

- Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18
The GCF is 6.

Step 2: Divide both by 6:
\[
\frac{12}{18} = \frac{12 \div 6}{18 \div 6} = \frac{2}{3}
\]

In simplest form: $\frac{12}{18} = \frac{2}{3}$

content.title: Simplifying step by step
content.solution:
If you don't see the GCF immediately, simplify in steps:
$$
\frac{24}{36} &= \frac{24 \div 2}{36 \div 2} = \frac{12}{18} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{12 \div 2}{18 \div 2} = \frac{6}{9} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{6 \div 3}{9 \div 3} = \frac{2}{3}
$$
Either way, $\frac{24}{36} = \frac{2}{3}$.

content.title: Cross-Multiplication Test
content.content:
Two fractions $\frac{a}{b}$ and $\frac{c}{d}$ are equivalent if and only 
if their cross-products are equal:
\[
\frac{a}{b} = \frac{c}{d} \iff a \times d = b \times c
\]

content.title: Testing equivalence
content.solution:
Cross-multiply:

- $3 \times 24 = 72$

- $8 \times 9 = 72$
Since $72 = 72$, the fractions are equivalent: $\frac{3}{8} = \frac{9}{24}$.

content.title: Non-equivalent fractions
content.solution:
Cross-multiply:

- $2 \times 12 = 24$

- $5 \times 5 = 25$
Since $24 \neq 25$, the fractions are not equivalent.

content.title: The Rhind Papyrus
content.content:
One of our best sources for ancient Egyptian mathematics is the Rhind 
Mathematical Papyrus, written around 1550 BCE by a scribe named Ahmes. 
It includes a table showing $\frac{2}{n}$ written as sums of unit 
fractions for all odd $n$ from 5 to 101. For example, $\frac{2}{5}$ was 
written as $\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{15}$. This elaborate table was a 
crucial reference for Egyptian mathematicians.

marginNotes.text:
For with the measure you use, it will be 
measured to you.

He has made everything beautiful in 
its time.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of sciences 
and number theory is the queen of mathematics.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The heart has its reasons which reason knows 
not.
